# Handoff: WebUSB transport for label printing

**For a fresh session.** Read this, then `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-27-web-label-printing.md`
(especially its "Hardware Verification Results" section) before doing anything.

## Where things stand

Branch `web-label-printing` (NOT merged to main; keep working here). The full web-label-printing
plan (8 tasks) is implemented, per-task reviewed (spec + quality, all approved), and final-reviewed.
`npm test` = 35/35 across 7 files; `npm run build` clean.

**Hardware-validated:** a job generated by the real `src/printing/` code (rasterCore →
brotherDriver bytes) physically printed on the PT-P710BT over a direct Bluetooth RFCOMM
connection. The pipeline is correct end-to-end.

**Broken (platform, not us):** macOS 26's virtual Bluetooth serial port silently drops writes
and never establishes the RFCOMM link, so Chrome's Web Serial — which sits on that port —
cannot reach the printer. (Brother's P-touch Editor greys its Bluetooth entry out as "USB only"
when USB is connected — that's USB-preference, not proof Bluetooth is unsupported on macOS.)
Full evidence trail in the plan doc's hardware-results section.

## The task

Add a **WebUSB transport** so the in-app Print button works over a USB cable, following the
same subagent-driven process as before (brainstorm → plan → implement with spec+quality reviews).

Sketch (from the plan doc, refine via brainstorming):
- `src/printing/webUsbTransport.ts` implementing the existing `Transport` interface
  (`open/write/read(timeoutMs, minBytes)/close`) over `navigator.usb`: requestDevice with
  vendor filter `0x04F9` (user gesture), claim interface, bulk OUT for job bytes, bulk IN for
  the 32-byte status. Driver/orchestrator/renderer need zero changes.
- Generalize `ptP710btProfile` (currently binds a `SerialPortLike`) or add a USB variant;
  decide how the Print button picks a transport (e.g. try USB, offer serial as fallback).
- Keep `webSerialTransport.ts` — it is spec-correct and works on platforms with functional
  SPP ports.

## Open questions to resolve first (in order)

> **RESOLVED 2026-07-18 — all three.** See `docs/hardware/pt-p710bt.md` for the full
> hardware/OS-layer reference. Summary: interface 0 is class 7/1/2 with bulk OUT 0x02 /
> bulk IN 0x81 (64-byte packets); Chrome claims it fine on macOS (no kernel driver in the
> way); no mode toggle needed — the status sequence answered correctly over WebUSB with a
> valid 32-byte reply (12mm media, no errors). Gotcha discovered: the printer auto-powers
> off when idle and disappears from enumeration — the transport/UI must surface
> "printer may be asleep" instead of failing opaquely.

1. **USB descriptor layout**: interface class/endpoints of the PT-P710BT. Enumerate via
   `ioreg -p IOUSB -l -w0` / a quick Chrome `navigator.usb` probe. Expect printer class (0x07)
   with bulk IN+OUT.
2. **Can Chrome claim it on macOS?** WebUSB fails if a kernel driver owns the interface.
   Signs are good (native P-touch app drives it from user space; no AppleUSBPrint match seen
   in ioreg), but verify empirically with `claimInterface()` before writing the full plan.
3. Does the USB path need a mode toggle on the printer (P700-family "Editor Lite" style)?
   Reportedly not for P710BT — confirm by successfully sending the status-request sequence
   (100×0x00, `1B 40`, `1B 69 53` → expect 32 bytes back, media width at byte 10).

## Hard constraints

- **Do not touch the Bluetooth pairing.** It's working and finicky; the user wants it left alone.
- No `@internal`/non-public API leakage into consumer code (standing rule).
- Keep the plan doc in sync with any code changes (established convention on this branch).
- Commits end with the `Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>` trailer.

## Useful artifacts

- `scripts/hardware-debug/` (committed on this branch):
  - `rfcomm_probe.swift` — Bluetooth status probe (works; proves printer + protocol).
  - `print_job.swift` — sends a job file over RFCOMM (this printed the validation label).
    Gotcha fixed inside: Swift `Data` slices keep parent indices — copy to `[UInt8]` first.
  - `genjob.ts` — builds a test job (outlined rectangle, 12mm) from the real driver code;
    run with `npx vite-node scripts/hardware-debug/genjob.ts`, writes `job.bin` next to itself.
  These are debug tools, not product code; they hardcode the printer's BT address and 12mm tape.
- Printer facts: USB vendor id `0x04F9`, product "PT-P710BT" (currently attached via a monitor's
  USB hub); BT SPP is RFCOMM channel 1; Brother 32-byte status: error bytes at offsets 8/9,
  media width (mm) at 10, full-status length 32 (`PrinterStatus.incomplete` = fewer than 32).
- Deferred follow-ups list (vertical squeeze fidelity, line rendering, mirroring check, etc.)
  lives in the plan doc's Self-Review Notes — none block the WebUSB work.

## Verification when done

Unit tests for the transport with a fake USB device (mirror `webSerialTransport.test.ts`'s
pattern), suite + build green, then a real print from the app in Chrome via the Print button
(USB picker). Orientation/mirroring on hardware is still an open watch-item from the original
plan — check it on the first real print.

### Hardware verification results (2026-07-18, end of day)

Implemented via `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-18-webusb-transport.md` (56 tests green,
build clean, per-task + final reviews). On-hardware, in Chrome via the Print button:

- **First print via USB picker: PASS** — picker listed PT-P710BT, label printed and auto-cut.
- **Zero-click reprint: PASS** — second print went through with no picker (granted-device path).
- **Asleep hint: PASS** — printer off → "may have auto-powered off" alert, no picker.
- Orientation/mirroring: printed labels look right in practice; a deliberate
  asymmetric-glyph check has not been done — still an open watch-item.
- Keepalive soak (>12 min idle with app open): **not yet run** — pending.
- New defect observed while testing (pre-existing, not transport-related): line objects
  render on screen as rectangles and print as horizontal lines regardless of their actual
  angle — tracked as a follow-up.
